findings.title: Docker pnpm store path breaks sandbox fallback with EACCES
findings.detail: buildPhaseArgv('install') always passes --store-dir /root/.pnpm-store to pnpm. This path is inside the Docker container image. When useFallback=true (Docker daemon unreachable), the same argv is passed to the host pnpm binary. Non-root host processes cannot write to /root/.pnpm-store, so pnpm install always fails with EACCES in fallback mode. The fallback that is supposed to keep verification working when Docker is down instead always reports an install failure.
findings.fix_sketch: When useFallback=true, omit --store-dir from the install argv so pnpm uses the default ~/.local/share/pnpm store.

findings.title: Timeout on fatal verify phase never reported as 'timeout' status
findings.detail: In the phase loop, isPhaseFatal() check fires a break before the timedOut check can run. For install, build, typecheck, and test (all fatal phases), a timeout sets outcome.timedOut=true but the isPhaseFatal break exits the loop first, leaving the outer timedOut flag false. computeStatus() therefore never returns 'timeout' for the vast majority of timeout scenarios; callers that key retry or notification on status==='timeout' never see it.
findings.fix_sketch: Move the timedOut check before the isPhaseFatal break in the phase loop body.

findings.title: Non-atomic SELECT+INSERT in store.insert() can throw UNIQUE constraint violation
findings.detail: insert() calls findByIdempotencyKey() (SELECT) then INSERT if not found. These are not wrapped in a transaction. Under concurrent multi-process access (PM2 restart overlap, drain() called concurrently), both processes can observe 'not found' and both attempt INSERT. The second INSERT throws SQLITE_CONSTRAINT_UNIQUE, propagating an unhandled exception to the caller rather than returning {inserted: false}. The drain() loop does not catch this and aborts mid-drain.
findings.fix_sketch: Replace the two-step logic with a single INSERT OR IGNORE and inspect result.changes to determine if the insert happened.

findings.title: store.close() skipped if cp.stop() throws during SIGTERM/SIGINT shutdown
findings.detail: shutdown() awaits cp.stop() before calling store.close(). If cp.stop() rejects (HTTP server close error), store.close() is never called, skipping the SQLite WAL checkpoint. The outer void also means the unhandled rejection may crash the process on Node 15+ instead of a clean exit. In WAL mode the last few writes may lose durability guarantees.
findings.fix_sketch: Wrap cp.stop() in try/finally to ensure store.close() always runs. Catch the overall shutdown promise to call process.exit(1) on error.

findings.title: markCompleted and markFailed call updateState without fromState guard
findings.detail: Both methods read state with getById then call updateState without the fromState parameter. Under multi-process SQLite access (PM2 restart overlap), both processes can execute their writes against the same row, overwriting each other's terminal state. Source notifications (GitHub label, Discord) are both sent, resulting in duplicate operator-visible messages.
findings.fix_sketch: Pass fromState: 'in_flight' to updateState in markCompleted and markFailed, treating result===false (no rows updated) as a no-op.

findings.title: scheduleTick swallows all tick() errors silently
findings.detail: this.tick(sprintId).catch(() => undefined) discards every error from the auto-loop tick. If tick() throws on every invocation (store corruption, introduced bug, corrupt task row), the sprint permanently stops advancing without any log entry. Operators have no signal that something is wrong until the sprint's age makes them investigate manually.
findings.fix_sketch: Log the error before discarding it: .catch((err) => console.error('[sprint] scheduleTick tick failed for', sprintId, ':', err));

findings.title: Raw SQLite verdict cast to PrVerdict without runtime validation
findings.detail: verdict: row.verdict as PrDecision['verdict'] casts the DB string to a typed union without checking membership. A manual DB edit or migration bug producing an invalid verdict silently succeeds and callers that switch on verdict hit unhandled branches. Only latent since current write paths are correctly typed.
findings.fix_sketch: Add a runtime guard: const VALID_VERDICTS = new Set(['merged','rejected','abandoned']); if (!VALID_VERDICTS.has(row.verdict)) throw new Error(...);

findings.title: classifyTask imported but never used in factory.ts
findings.detail: src/pipeline/factory.ts imports classifyTask from the classifier module at line 11 but never calls it in production code (only appears in a comment). ESLint flags this as a lint error. The routing strategy now goes through routingStrategy.classify(), making the direct import redundant.
findings.fix_sketch: Remove classifyTask from the import at line 11.

findings.title: build-cards.ts accesses private TaskStore.db via unsafe as-any cast
findings.detail: readAllDecisions() reaches into the TaskStore via (store as any).db to run a cross-repo query. This bypasses TypeScript encapsulation and breaks if the property is renamed or if the class is mocked in tests. The comment acknowledges the issue but marks it out-of-scope. This should be fixed by adding a getAllPrDecisions() public method to TaskStore.
findings.fix_sketch: Add getAllPrDecisions(limit?: number): PrDecision[] to TaskStore, then call it from readAllDecisions() instead of reaching into .db directly.
